fund.name: ICICI Prudential Large Cap Fund (erstwhile Bluechip Fund) - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Core large-cap equity holding — the backbone of the portfolio. ICICI Prudential Large Cap Fund is a well-established, low-cost passive-like fund with ~12 years of track record and a simple mandate: buy India's top 30 companies and hold them. At 0.20% yearly fee, it's one of the lowest-cost options available even among active funds. This fund captures the long-run 12-14% annual return India's markets have historically delivered, while keeping costs minimal. Equity-taxed (LTCG 12.5% on gains above ₹1.25L after 1 year), this is the wealth-building engine for Katniss's 30-year horizon.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option
fund.display_name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: A passive index fund tracking the Nifty Midcap 150 — 150 mid-sized Indian companies that are growing faster than large-cap companies. This is the growth kicker in the portfolio. With a low 0.20% yearly fee, this is the cheapest way to access mid-cap growth. Important note: mid-caps are more volatile (they can drop 40-50% in bad years) so this is appropriate only because Katniss has a 30-year horizon and a buffer of salary + emergency fund. Over 30 years, mid-caps historically deliver superior returns to large-caps. Equity-taxed — same tax advantage as the large-cap fund.

plan.setup_phase: Month 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on MF Central or Zerodha Coin or Groww (avoid third-party platforms — they charge higher fees on SIP installments). Set up two SIP mandates: ₹6,400/month into ICICI Large Cap and ₹1,600/month into Motilal Midcap 150. Set both to auto-debit on the 5th of every month from your bank account. Deposit ₹50,000 as a lumpsum into ICICI Large Cap first (₹25,000) and Motilal Midcap 150 (₹10,000) — this is your starting corpus. Do NOT use a debt fund for the emergency fund yet — see below.
review_checkpoints: Month 3: Confirm both SIP mandates running. Check your confirmation emails + MF Central dashboard. If either SIP is delayed, fix it immediately — missing one month compounds over 30 years.
Year 1 (Month 12): Check total corpus, SIP step-up (increase both SIPs by 10%), review emergency fund progress. If emergency fund not fully built by Year 1, adjust SIPs temporarily.
Year 3: Marriage goal review — estimate how much you'll need to save for marriage (₹5L at 2025 price ~₹7-8L at 2031). If you're behind, increase SIPs by an extra 5% this year.
Year 5: First major review with a full portfolio check. Reassess goals, income, and if the allocation (80/20) still makes sense.
Year 10 and Year 20: Formal portfolio review — rebalance if drift >5%, check if salary growth allows increasing SIPs further.
Age 53 (Year 25): Begin gradual de-risking — start shifting some money into a debt fund for the marriage corpus (which should now be 5-7 years away).
Age 58 (Year 27): Near retirement. Shift 40% to debt, 30% to equity, 30% to gold — protect what you've built.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Check your portfolio ONCE a year — ideally on your birthday or anniversary. Look at the current equity % vs target (80/20). If equity has grown to >85% because one fund performed much better, sell a tiny bit of the winning fund and buy more of the lagging one to bring it back to 80/20. NEVER rebalance based on market news, fund manager changes, or any guru's prediction. Rebalancing should be a mechanical, calendar-based event. If you're not able to do this, ignore it — the drift doesn't hurt you meaningfully over 30 years.
plan.rationale: Katniss is 23 — that is the single most valuable position in investing. Time is her asset, not money. The plan is built on three principles: (1) Keep it simple — only two funds, both passive/index-based with near-zero active management fees; (2) Start small and increase — ₹8,000/month today is all that's needed, and it grows 10% every year; (3) Tax efficiency — every rupee in the plan is equity-taxed at 12.5% LTCG above ₹1.25L, which is the most tax-efficient category for an Indian investor in the new regime. She has a 30-year roadmap, so even small monthly amounts compound into meaningful numbers. The 80/20 split (large-cap index + mid-cap index) is broad, diversified, and appropriate for a moderate risk appetite over 30 years. The emergency fund is built separately (outside the mutual fund plan) to keep the MF portfolio fully invested — never use a mutual fund as an emergency fund.
risks: Market risk: Indian markets can fall 30-40% in a bad year (e.g., 2008, 2020). Your portfolio will drop sharply — this is normal. Do NOT panic and redeem. Stay invested.

Sequence-of-returns risk: If markets crash in your early 20s (when you're saving aggressively) vs. in your late 50s (when you're withdrawing), the total corpus can differ by lakhs. This plan assumes markets recover long-term.
Inflation risk:₹50,000 today is worth much less in 30 years. The plan assumes the corpus grows well above inflation — historically equity has done this.
Currency risk: Gold allocation is in rupees, so this is not a valid concern for Katniss.
Behavioural risk: The biggest risk is reacting to market news/trends. Staying on the SIP step-up and annual review schedule is the single most important habit.
Liquidity risk: If the emergency fund is not built, you might be forced to redeem mutual funds in a bad market — avoid this by building ₹3-4L in a savings account first.
Fee drag: A 0.20% fee on ₹8,000/month today seems small, but over 30 years it can cost lakhs in lost returns. This plan uses the lowest-cost options available.
Tax mismatch risk: Even though the plan is equity-taxed, partial redemptions before 1 year trigger slab tax (20% or 30% depending on bracket) on gains. Plan all redemptions for tax years carefully.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase total SIP by 10% every year to match salary growth. At ₹8,000 today, this grows to ~₹17,000/month by Year 7 — dramatically accelerating wealth building on the back of your salary increases.

allocation_schedule.trigger: Starting year — 100% equity for maximum long-run wealth growth
6-year mark — marriage corpus goal check. If ₹5L+ saved in equity, move marriage corpus to a debt fund (short-duration) for safety. Keep overall portfolio at 80/20.
Mid-decade review — confirm continued growth. No major changes needed. Consider adding a small gold ETF if gold allocation feels incomplete.
Age ~42-44 — 15 years into the plan. Begin gradual de-risking. Shift 15% to debt and 5% to gold. Marriage corpus now fully safe.
Age 53-54 — within 10 years of retirement. Significantly reduce equity exposure. Debt and gold now protect the corpus. Assess retirement income needs.
Age 58 — 5 years from retirement. Shift to a defensive, conservative allocation. Equity only for long-term growth beyond retirement.

plan.perspective: Katniss, you're 23 and already thinking about your future — that's exactly what you should be doing. At this age, your most powerful tool is time, not money. ₹8,000/month today, kept growing by 10% every year, can become ₹50-70 lakhs in 30 years depending on markets. The plan is built for simplicity: two low-cost funds, both passive, one auto-debit SIP, and no complicated switches. The only thing you need to remember is to increase your SIP every year — even by just ₹800 — and review once a year on your birthday. That's it. Everything else will take care of itself.

aps.reasoning: The plan is highly passive overall. For passive_instrument_fraction: 20% is in a pure index fund (Nifty Midcap 150), while 80% is in an actively managed large-cap fund that's described as 'passive-like' with low tracking error and simple mandate, scoring 0.6. Turnover_score is 0.9 due to annual rebalancing only and explicit instructions against frequent trading. Cost_emphasis_score is 0.8 as expense ratios (0.2%) are highlighted as important but not the sole criterion. Research_vs_cost_score is 0.9 as the plan relies on broad market exposure rather than individual security analysis. Time_horizon_alignment_score is 1.0 with 30-year horizon and long-term compounding focus. Portfolio_activeness_score is 0.8 because while the large-cap fund is active, it's described as passive-like with low costs, and mid-cap is pure index, making the overall portfolio relatively passive.

pqs.reasoning: The plan demonstrates excellent alignment with Katniss's goals (30-year horizon, marriage in 6 years) and tax efficiency (properly optimized for new_regime investor with equity-taxed funds). Diversification is good but could be better with broader asset class coverage beyond just large/mid-cap equity. Risk-return profile is appropriate for moderate risk appetite over long horizon. The plan is highly consistent throughout with clear rationale, projections, and rebalancing guidelines that support the strategy.
